coredata.dc:title: Electrospun nanofibers membrane of carbon quantum dots loaded onto chitosan-polyvinyl alcohol for removal of rhodamine B dye from aqueous solutions: Adsorption isotherm, kinetics, thermodynamics and optimization via Box-Behnken design

coredata.dc:description: The study aims to blend carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with chitosan and polyvinyl alcohol to produce micro fibrous membranes. To create electrospun carbon quantum dots nanofibers (ECQNF) that can absorb rhodamine B dye (RB) found in wastewater. A variety of methodologies were utilized to investigate the ECQNF, including scanning electron microscopy (SEM) for surface morphology, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) to identify functional groups, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) for elemental analysis, X-ray diffraction (XRD), and N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms for structural analysis. Batch adsorption experiments varied parameters like contact time, initial RB concentrations, and adsorbent dosages. The membrane achieved a maximum adsorption of 522 mg/g at 600 ppm and 298 K. Thermodynamic evaluation indicated the process is endothermic. Several isothermal models analyzed the adsorption of RB onto the electrospun adsorbent, with the Langmuir model best fitting the data. The pseudo-second-order model effectively described the kinetics. The Dubinin-Radushkevich model indicated an adsorption energy of 32.6 kJ/mol, suggesting chemisorption. Positive entropy and enthalpy values, along with a more negative Gibbs free energy, confirm the process is endothermic and spontaneous. The increase in adsorption capability with rising temperature is evident. The ECQNF can be reused up to six times, maintaining efficiency and chemical structure. The XRD analyses demonstrate consistent results before and after each reuse cycle. It's crucial to investigate the interaction mechanisms between the adsorbent and adsorbate, including hydrogen bonding, n-π stacking, electrostatic interactions, and pore filling. Using the Box-Behnken design, the adsorption outcomes were optimized, highlighting the effectiveness of ECQNF in enhancing pollutant removal from wastewater.

head.ce:title: Electrospun nanofibers membrane of carbon quantum dots loaded onto chitosan-polyvinyl alcohol for removal of rhodamine B dye from aqueous solutions: Adsorption isotherm, kinetics, thermodynamics and optimization via Box-Behnken design

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study aims to blend carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with chitosan and polyvinyl alcohol to produce micro fibrous membranes. To create electrospun carbon quantum dots nanofibers (ECQNF) that can absorb rhodamine B dye (RB) found in wastewater. A variety of methodologies were utilized to investigate the ECQNF, including scanning electron microscopy (SEM) for surface morphology, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) to identify functional groups, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) for elemental analysis, X-ray diffraction (XRD), and N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms for structural analysis. Batch adsorption experiments varied parameters like contact time, initial RB concentrations, and adsorbent dosages. The membrane achieved a maximum adsorption of 522 mg/g at 600 ppm and 298 K. Thermodynamic evaluation indicated the process is endothermic. Several isothermal models analyzed the adsorption of RB onto the electrospun adsorbent, with the Langmuir model best fitting the data. The pseudo-second-order model effectively described the kinetics. The Dubinin-Radushkevich model indicated an adsorption energy of 32.6 kJ/mol, suggesting chemisorption. Positive entropy and enthalpy values, along with a more negative Gibbs free energy, confirm the process is endothermic and spontaneous. The increase in adsorption capability with rising temperature is evident. The ECQNF can be reused up to six times, maintaining efficiency and chemical structure. The XRD analyses demonstrate consistent results before and after each reuse cycle. It's crucial to investigate the interaction mechanisms between the adsorbent and adsorbate, including hydrogen bonding, n-π stacking, electrostatic interactions, and pore filling. Using the Box-Behnken design, the adsorption outcomes were optimized, highlighting the effectiveness of ECQNF in enhancing pollutant removal from wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All relevant data are within the manuscript and available from the corresponding author upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: The advancement and increasing complexity of industrial processes have unavoidably resulted in the release of significant amounts of waste into the surrounding environment. Inadequate handling of these toxic substances ultimately leads to serious repercussions for both human health and ecological systems. Dyes, in particular, represent a unique category of pollutants, they are characterized by their resistance to biological degradation and their exceptional stability, rendering them a particularly challenging environmental contaminant [1]. There is a significant demand for an efficient, environmentally sustainable, and economically viable approach to eliminate dyes from wastewater. Over the past several decades, a variability of physical and chemical behavior methods have been implemented to address dye-laden wastewater. However, these techniques often incur substantial costs and frequently fall short of achieving the requisite zero discharge emission standards, as well as the ongoing treatment needs associated with large-scale industrial wastewater [2]. The ongoing research aims to develop methods that achieve 100 % efficiency while meeting industrial standards. The textile industry's rapid changes have caused in the statement of a growing amount of dyes into natural bodies of water, significantly contributing to water pollution. The presence of these dyes not only reduces water transparency but also hampers photosynthesis, resulting in decreased oxygen levels [3,4]. Consumption of water contaminated with dyes can be fatal to fish. Moreover, dyed water suffers from low transmittance, posing serious risks to human health and leading to substantial waste from traditional textile dyeing processes. In this context, the modification of nanofibers, an innovative class of nanomaterial, primarily entails the immobilization of diverse nanomaterials onto the fiber structures [5,6]. The incorporation of these nanomaterials provides numerous advantages, including a substantial specific surface area, enhanced chemical and mechanical belongings, as well as a variety of functional characteristics. Nanofibers enhanced with nanomaterials have applications beyond wastewater management, garnering attention for their unique characteristics. Various studies demonstrate that nanofibers integrated with functional nanoparticles exhibit potential antiviral, antimicrobial, and other specialized properties [7].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), derived from carbon-based substrates, exhibit remarkable photoluminescent characteristics along with non-toxic, eco-friendly nature, and substantial photostability. These attributes make CQDs particularly advantageous in applications such as light emission technologies, photovoltaic systems, bioimaging methodologies, and oncological therapies. Additionally, CQDs demonstrate potential in the adsorption procedure for the extraction of pollutant dyes from wastewater, highlighting their multifunctional utility in environmental remediation. Additionally, compounds such as glycine, citric acid, urea, ethylene diamine, polyethylene glycol, and other polymers can serve as carbon and nitrogen sources for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The CQDs produced exhibit remarkable optical properties that vary depending on the source of the polymers or precursors used [8,9]. A straightforward method for the one-step synthesis of nitrogen-doped CQDs, accompanied by a distinctive modification process, has been successfully developed. Several factors must be taken into account when synthesizing CQDs, including the choice of precursors and reactants, this includes the reaction's pH, duration, and temperature [10].

The incorporation of chitosan and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) in the electrospinning process for adsorption demonstrates a range of notable benefits. These advantages contribute to the improved efficacy of the resultant nanofibers, positioning them as highly effective adsorbents for applications in environmental remediation [11]. Chitosan and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) exhibit biocompatibility and non-toxicity, attributes that render them appropriate for applications in water treatment, particularly where safety and reduced environmental impact are of utmost importance. The synergistic application of these polymers through the electrospinning process facilitates the creation of nanofibers characterized by substantial surface areas and porosity. This structural enhancement is pivotal in augmenting the adsorption capabilities of these nanofibers against various contaminants, notably heavy metals and organic dyes [12,13]. Chitosan introduces amino and hydroxyl functional groups, which significantly improve its ability to interact with a range of contaminants, especially cationic dyes and metal ions. In contrast, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) offers structural stability and mechanical durability, culminating in resilient adsorbent materials that preserve their functionality across diverse environmental conditions. The electrospinning technique enables the generation of uniform nanofibers, allowing precise regulation of fiber structure and diameter, crucial for maximizing adsorption efficiency. The combination of chitosan and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is adjustable, enhancing adsorption properties and improving effectiveness against specific contaminants. The integration of chitosan and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) may enhance the overall effectiveness of adsorption processes. This improvement arises from chitosan's capacity to chelate metal ions, which is further supported by the structural properties of PVA. Additionally, both materials originate from renewable sources and possess biodegradable properties, reflecting the increasing trend towards the use of sustainable materials in the realm of wastewater treatment. The ability to integrate supplementary materials, such as nanoparticles or other adsorbents, into the electrospinning process facilitates the creation of hybrid nanofibers with improved characteristics. This capability underscores the effectiveness and sustainability of utilizing chitosan and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) in electrospinning as a method for eliminating pollutants from wastewater [14].

Numerous experimental investigations have been conducted regarding the elimination of organic dyes from water using diverse adsorbents. This report focuses on five innovative assay parameters. Initially, chitosan-polyvinyl alcohol was assessed as an adsorbent for the inaugural time with Rhodamine B dye. Additionally, while carbon quantum dots have been recognized for their efficacy in the adsorption of various substances, there has been a notable absence of data concerning their application with Rhodamine B dye [15,16]. An established approach is the third method utilized in electro-spinning, which facilitates the generation of uniform continuous polymer nanofibers. The utilization of membrane technologies, particularly ultrafiltration, can prove effective for the large-scale manufacture of films composed of these nanofibers on extensive surface areas. Fourth, full factorial design is often used to improve a range of variables linked to assay parameters. Furthermore, the Box-Behnken design permits the simultaneous omission of all factors at predetermined levels: high, medium, or low. Compared to other commonly used models, this method enables the execution of multiple trial batches, suggesting that having an excess of adsorbents, which could lead to unavoidable expenses, does not indicate a deficiency in the overall model. The CQDs-CS-PVA-EF membrane has been utilized for the treatment of wastewater from diverse origins, classifications, and treatment approaches. Therefore, this study aims to be structured through a comprehensive analysis incorporating theoretical, experimental, and practical assessments, all informed by the principles of response surface methodology [17]. The incorporation of carbon quantum dots into the chitosan film enhanced the membrane's synthesis compared to a standard chitosan film. These fibrous membranes demonstrated exceptional efficacy in adsorbing rhodamine B dye from water. Key mechanisms involved in adsorption include electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and conjugated π-π interactions. Furthermore, the optical properties of the carbon quantum dots propose that electron transfer during the adsorption procedure plays a character in the quenching of rhodamine B dye [18]. The ability to reuse these membranes indicates that the carbon quantum dots-chitosan-polyvinyl alcohol fibrous membrane serves as a sustainable option for wastewater treatment. Rhodamine B dye is an organic dye that poses toxicity, carcinogenicity, and mutagenicity risks, and it is extensively utilized in various environments, impacting both human health and ecosystems negatively. Adsorption is commonly acknowledged as one of the most efficient techniques for eliminating hazardous dyes from water solutions. The process of adsorbing rhodamine B dye onto electrospun nanofiber membranes made from biomaterials has been infrequently documented in research. The efficacy of pure chitosan electrospun nanofibers has been found lacking. In response, researchers initially utilized chitosan-polyvinyl alcohol bi-component nanofibers for the adsorptive elimination of rhodamine B dye. The favorable compatibility within the polyelectrospinning system allows these chitosan-polyvinyl alcohol nanofibers to possess a substantial specific surface area, a porous architecture, and a high degree of hydrophilicity. Furthermore, they feature robust macromolecular binding sites attributable to the attendance of –OH and –NH2 functional groups [19].

Because it lowers the concentration of solutes in the solution, adsorption is the primary process used in wastewater treatment. Numerous physical, chemical, and biological water treatment techniques used in wastewater treatment facilities depend on this process. Research on the use of chitosan fiber for adsorption has demonstrated its high efficiency. Additionally, carbon dot-modified adsorbents have been extensively assessed for their effectiveness in extracting pollutants, benefiting from the diverse functional groups present [20]. In order to broaden the scope of carbon dot applications, researchers formulated an electrospun nanofiber membrane incorporating carbon quantum dots embedded within a chitosan-polyvinyl alcohol matrix [21]. This innovative membrane was utilized as a prototype for the extraction of rhodamine B dye from contaminated water sources. The investigation focused on assessing various adsorption characteristics, including the volume of flooding, duration of wetting, swelling time, water retention capacity, fire resistance, and the effectiveness of the membrane in eliminating rhodamine B dye from wastewater. The membrane demonstrated strong adsorption capabilities for rhodamine B dye, which was assessed in relation to its effectiveness in pollutant removal from wastewater [22].

An analytical investigation utilizing Design of Experimentation through response surface methodology was conducted to examine the influence of specified limits on achieving optimal removal levels [23,24]. To facilitate this exploration, a Box-Behnken Design was employed to identify the ideal conditions for removal while also assessing the interactive effects among the variables involved [25,26]. The Box-Behnken Design serves as a systematic approach developed by constructing a series of empirical models based on experimental data gathered from designated trials. The study focused on optimizing the removal of rhodamine B (RB) dye utilizing carbon quantum dots (CQD) in conjunction with a chitosan-polyvinyl alcohol (C-PVA) nanofibrous membrane [27]. The investigation measured the impact of various operating conditions on RB dye removal, specifically examining the initial concentration of RB, the duration of interaction, and the mass of CQD incorporated within the CS-PVA membrane. Analysis indicated that the efficiency of RB dye removal was directly proportional to all assessed operational parameters [28].

This research presents a novel approach in the synthesis of a new nanofiber composed of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), chitosan, and polyvinyl alcohol utilizing electrospinning techniques. The primary objective is to achieve efficient removal of industrial dyes, specifically rhodamine B (RB). The study comprehensively examines various factors influencing the adsorption process through batch experiments, focusing on the roles of concentration, contact time, and thermodynamic parameters. Response surface methodology (RSM) was employed to analyze the influence of chosen parameters on the efficacy of removal processes. This methodology was utilized to identify the optimal conditions for removal and to evaluate the interactions among the various factors involved. To support this analysis, a Box-Behnken design was implemented. Validation of the model demonstrated a strong alignment between the observed data and the predicted outcomes. RSM possesses wide-ranging applicability across numerous practical fields, notably in the evaluation of liquid quality in mixtures.
Detailed descriptions of the materials used for each detail and instrument are included as visual representations in the supplementary materials, specifically in (Table S1 and S2).

The hydrothermal process was employed to synthesize Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs). In this process, 5 g of citric acid served as the carbon source, while an equivalent amount of urea, also 5 g, acted as the nitrogen precursor. These components were dissolved in 50 ml of deionized distilled water. Subsequently, the resultant mixture was placed into a 150-ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After that, the autoclave was heated to 150 °C and kept there for five hours [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of electrospun carbon quantum dots nanofibers (ECQNF)
ce:section.ce:para: Nanofiber scaffolds were created using the electrospinning technique. Specifically, a solution consisting of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) at a concentration of 10 % w/v in 90 % acetic acid was combined with a chitosan solution at 2 % w/v in the same solvent, maintaining a volume ratio of 70:30 (PVA:CS). This mixed polymer solution was then introduced into a glass syringe attached to a NANON-01B electrospinner (MECC Nanofiber) and delivered through a 20-gauge blunt-tip needle by a syringe pump operating at a flow rate of 0.5 mL per hour. An elevated voltage supply, operating at 27 kV, was utilized to generate an electric field across a blunt-tip needle, situated 15 cm away from a grounded collector plate, as depicted in Fig. 1 . To incorporate carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into the nanofibers, a composite of chitosan (CS) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) in a 5 % weight ratio was prepared as (ECQNF). This mixture underwent thorough blending followed by spinning, after which the aforementioned procedures were reiterated [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal and adsorption studies of RB via ECQNF
ce:section.ce:para:
Adsorption experiments were conducted utilizing a batch process methodology. Solutions of dye were organized by dissolving rhodamine B dye (RB) in distilled water to obtain designated concentrations. Subsequently, adsorbents with pre-measured weights were introduced into the dye solutions [23,24]. The dosage of the ECQNF adsorbent ranged from 0.02 to 0.25 g per 25 mL of solution, while the pH of the solution was varied between 2.0 and 12.0. Additionally, the initial concentration of dye in the solution was set from 25 to 600 ppm. Temperature conditions were tested from 20 to 45 °C were also assessed. The pH of the dye solutions was modified using both sodium hydroxide (0.01 M) and hydrochloric acid (0.01 M) to achieve the desired levels. This experimental setup aimed to systematically evaluate how these parameters influence the removal efficiency of RB dye [29,30]. The influence of ionic strength on the adsorption capability was assessed by maintaining the optimized investigational parameters, which involved examining the adsorption capabilities of ECQNF across various concentrations of NaCl solutions, ranging since 0.1 to 0.5 mol/L. Adsorption experiments were conducted by agitating the samples for 100 min at a constant temperature. After this period, the concentrations of dyes in the solutions were resolute by measuring the absorbance at 554 nm utilizing the UV–Vis spectrophotometric method for the RB dye. The evaluation of adsorption capacity (qe) and the efficiency of dye removal were carried out using Eqs. (1) and (2):

Statistical models are formulated through the application of Response Surface Methodology (RSM) to address difficulties characterized by multiple solutions. The fundamental objective is to elucidate the relationship between the response variable and the various experimental factors. This methodology also facilitates enhancements in processes. RSM systematically employs a series of designed experiments that leverage the inherent characteristics of the process to identify the optimal solution across the entire operational framework. Central Composite Design (CCD) represents a widely utilized methodology for the optimization of process parameters. Eq. 1 quantifies the amount of RB dye absorbed per gram of adsorbent, precisely the ECQNF, over different time intervals (t). Otherwise, Eq. 2 can be employed to evaluate the efficacy of removal [23,24]. Three variables were identified as critical parameters influencing the process: “adsorbent weight”, “pH of the solution”, and “contact time”. These parameters were chosen due to their negative impact on the adsorption capability, as detailed in Table S3.

Table S3 presents a full overview of the maximum and minimum values for every parameter, developed using Design Expert Software. This effectively demonstrates the various combinations of limits alongside their respective productions. It comprises P center runs, “2 × n” axial runs, and 2n factorial runs, thereby encompassing a range of experimental designs and conditions [25]. The quantity of experimental trials required, which is contingent upon the number of contribution variables, can be calculated utilizing Eq. (3):

“N” represents the quantity of procedure variables that impact the outcomes, whereas “p” indicates the total number of required experimental trials. In this research, the value assigned to “m” is 3 [31]. The Central Composite design encompasses three essential phases: determining the coefficients of the model, establishing the experimental framework, and forecasting the model's performance while conducting a thorough analysis of the outcomes. Upon completion of the preceding work, an empirical model has been developed to assess the function's behavior in relation to various combinations of contribution variables. As a result, a quadratic reversion model is formulated, as illustrated in Eq. (4):

In this context, let us define “I” as representing the resistance coefficient and “j” as the speed coefficient. The coefficients associated with resistance, interaction, speed, and the constant term are expressed as β0, βi, βii, and βij, respectively. The efficiency of the proposed polynomial model equation was evaluated utilizing R2, R2 Adj, and R2 Pred metrics [24,32]. A higher R2 value signifies an enhanced alignment of the calculation with the investigational data, thereby indicating improved accuracy in the model's predictive capability [33].

Fig. 2(a) illustrates the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns corresponding to carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a chitosan/polyvinyl alcohol (CS/PVA) membrane, and the composite ECQNF membrane (CQDs@CS/PVA). The investigation of the XRD data reveals that the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) displayed a distinct broad peak at 18.83 degrees, which is associated with the (002) plane according to JCPDS card no. 26–1076. This peak is also discernible in the XRD pattern of the ECQNF membrane, providing compelling evidence for the incorporation of the CQDs within the composite membrane structure, thereby confirming their successful synthesis [21].

The nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms for both ECQNF and RB@ECQNF present a Type IV isotherm, which includes a hysteresis loop, which is typical of mesoporous resources with pore sizes reaching from 2 to 50 nm. Prior to the adsorption of RB dye, ECQNF demonstrates a surface area of 162.84 m2/g, a pore size of 6.4 nm, and a pore volume of 2.6 cm3/g. These measurements suggest that ECQNF possesses a distinctly well-developed mesoporous structure. The isotherm illustrates a progressive increase in adsorption at low comparative pressures (P/P₀ < 0.3), which can be attributed to the formation of a monolayer [34–36]. This is succeeded by a more pronounced incline observed at medium pressures (0.3 < P/P₀ < 0.8), indicative of contributions from multilayer adsorption and capillary condensation phenomena. In the area of higher relative pressures (P/P₀ > 0.8), the data indicates a steep escalation in adsorption, corresponding to the filling of mesopores and macropores. Following the adsorption of RB, there is a notable reduction in the surface area to 118.44 m2/g, with pore size decreasing to 4.8 nm and pore volume to 1.82 cm3/g. This outcome signifies the effective incorporation of RB molecules into the mesoporous structure, resulting in partial obstruction of the pores and a consequent decline in overall porosity (Fig. 2(b)). Additionally, the observation of a smaller hysteresis loop after the adsorption process serves to further validate the observed decrease in mesoporosity, reinforcing the material's potential utility in adsorption-related applications [37,38].

The UV absorption spectrum of the synthesized aqueous carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are presented in Fig. 2(c). The UV absorption spectrum of the aqueous carbon quantum dots (CQDs) solution display two notable absorption bands, a relatively minor band associated with the π-π* transition of electrons in aromatic structures at 235 nm, and a wider peak at 342 nm linked to the n-π* transition of CO groups. The broad peak observed at 342 nm in the UV absorption spectra of the carbon is attributed to the significant capture of excitation energy, which results in a considerable emission at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

Fig. 2(d) presents the Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), displaying absorption bands at 3471, 3196, 1676, 1425, and 906 cm−1. These specific bands indicate the attendance of numerous surface functional groups associated with the CQDs, thereby providing insights into their chemical characteristics. The absorption band observed at 3471 cm−1 is qualified to stretching vibrations associated with OH and NH bonds. In addition, the band detected at 3002 cm−1 is ascribed to the bending vibrations of CH groups. The spectral bands located at 1676, 1425, and 906 cm−1 can be qualified to the vibrational modes of C-O-C linkages, CH2 groups, and CN bonds, respectively. Consequently, the findings derived from the FT-IR analysis suggest that the surfaces of the carbon dots exhibit a predominantly hydrophilic character, characterized by the attendance of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amine functional groups. The hydrophilic properties of the surface likely account for its effective dispersibility in water. The analysis of the FT-IR spectrum of the CS-PVA nanofiber membrane, thereby maintaining the stability of the electrospinning process. The prominent OH (3567 cm−1) and NH (3249 cm−1) stretching bands reveal the persistence of hydrogen bonding, which plays a crucial role in modulating the viscosity of the solution and ensuring the stability of the jet during electrospinning. The detection of CH stretching peaks at 2930 and 2746 cm−1 affirms the preservation of the polymer backbone's structural integrity, which is vital in limiting excessive charge buildup. Furthermore, the observation of the CO stretching peak at 1732 cm−1 indicates that there is no evidence of degradation or undesired crosslinking, thereby safeguarding the mechanical characteristics of the fibers. Moreover, the identification of CO stretching (1255 cm−1) and aromatic CH bending (753 cm−1) provides additional confirmation of the structural stability within the nanofiber matrix. Collectively, these results substantiate that the optimized parameters for electrospinning have been effective in managing the process, even with the enhanced conductivity introduced by the carbon dots. Finally, the FT-IR spectrum for ECQNF reveals several distinctive spectral features indicative of its molecular structure. Notably, the attendance of CH stretching is observed at 2926 cm−1, while the carbonyl group's CO stretching appears at 1726 cm−1. Additionally, C-CH3 stretching is identified at 1353 cm−1, and the asymmetric stretching of the ester group, C-O-C, is noted at 1260 cm−1. Furthermore, significant peaks at 1442 cm−1 (C=C), 1239 cm−1 (O–C=O), 1086 cm−1 (C–O–C), and 933 cm−1 (CN) provide evidence for the attendance of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within the sample [32].

The characterization of synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was conducted using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to assess their morphology and particle size in a water dispersion. As illustrated in Fig. 2(e), the TEM image indicates that the as-synthesized CQDs exhibit a consistent particle size distribution and display a well-defined spherical morphology. The particle size distribution exhibits an average range between 1.5 and 4 nm. The diffraction pattern obtained from the selected area of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is demonstrated in Fig. 2(f) [31].

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) illustrates a complex morphology characterized by porosity and a layered structure, accompanied by uneven surface textures and interconnected network formations. The observed surface characteristics exhibit a pattern of voids and pores, suggesting an elevated surface area, which is essential for potential applications in adsorption, as depicted in Fig. 2(g). The porous characteristics noted in the material contribute to improved engagement with molecules or ions, indicating superior capabilities in both adsorption and diffusion. The structure of the CQDs material seems to be composed of conglomerated carbonaceous layers, resulting in a textured and irregular surface profile. The presence of these interlinked pores and fissures facilitates effective molecular movement and enhances surface accessibility. This characteristic is advantageous for various applications, including adsorption processes and environmental remediation efforts. The observable micro-scale architecture, as represented by the 1 μm scale bar, underscores the material's homogeneity and structural reliability throughout the examined region. This observation validates the material's significant porosity and nanoscale attributes, which are critical for its functional applications [34].

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of the ECQNF membrane depicts a tightly interconnected architecture of uniformly sized nanofibers that construct a highly porous, web-like formation. The consistent diameters of the nanofibers, as indicated by the 5 μm scale bar, contribute to the formation of a uniformly distributed porous network. This configuration is significant as it facilitates superior permeability and effective transport routes for molecules or ions. The intermingling fibers contribute to the membrane's mechanical robustness and adaptability, thereby rendering it appropriate for conditions that require dynamic operation, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . The presence of smooth fiber surfaces, characterized by negligible imperfections such as bead formation, indicates a superior quality of electrospinning [12]. The Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum, along with the pie chart representing the composition of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), reveals that carbon constitutes the majority of the material at 56.4 %. This is followed by nitrogen, which accounts for 33.5 %, and oxygen, which makes up 10.1 %. These findings underscore the carbon-rich character of the CQDs, while also emphasizing the notable levels of nitrogen and oxygen incorporated within the structure, as depicted in Fig. 2(h). The elevated carbon concentration indicates a strong structural framework in carbon quantum dots (CQDs), ensuring their structural integrity and superior electrical conductivity. Additionally, the presence of nitrogen suggests the existence of functional groups, such as amines or nitriles, which are instrumental in improving chemical reactivity and enhancing adsorption characteristics. The presence of oxygen, linked to hydroxyl or carboxyl functional groups, enhances the hydrophilicity and provides active sites for molecular interactions, thereby augmenting their applicability [29].

The SEM mapping combined with EDX analysis of the ECQNF membrane post-Rhodamine B (RB) dye adsorption indicates the presence of a nanofibrous architecture, characterized by discernible clusters of RB particles adhering to the fibers. This observation corroborates the effective adsorption of the dye. The elemental mapping indicates a consistent distribution of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) across the sample. Notably, nitrogen, represented in yellow, constitutes the predominant element, comprising 64.3 % of the composition. This is followed by carbon, depicted in blue, which accounts for 20 %, and oxygen, shown in orange, making up 15.7 %. This distribution is further corroborated by the EDX spectrum presented in Fig. 2(i). The elevated nitrogen content underscores the significance of nitrogen-rich functional groups, such as amines or nitriles, in improving the adsorption of RB via mechanisms like electrostatic interactions or hydrogen bonding. Simultaneously, the presence of oxygen-containing groups provides supplementary sites for adsorption. The consistent distribution of carbon indicates the carbonaceous characteristics of the membrane, contributing to its stable and porous structure. The SEM and EDX analyses provide substantial evidence for the ECQNF membrane's significant adsorption capacity. The presence of specific functional groups, coupled with its porous architecture, contributes to its high efficacy in dye removal and applications related to environmental remediation [30].

The XPS analysis of the ECQNF reveals critical insights into the elemental makeup of the material's surface. Noteworthy peaks corresponding to carbon (C1s), oxygen (O1s), and nitrogen (N1s) suggest the existence of a functionalized carbon-based architecture. The C1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of ECQNF exhibits three notable peaks that represent various chemical states of carbon, thereby given that insight into the material's surface configuration. The primary peak observed at 285.44 eV, which constitutes 71.52 % of the total carbon detected, is associated with sp2-hybridized carbon. This includes species such as CC and CH bonds, suggesting that the material has a predominantly graphitic or aromatic structure, characteristic of hydrocarbon frameworks, as illustrated in Fig. 4 . The secondary peak observed at 287 eV, which accounts for 23.36 % of the total, is indicative of carbon atoms that are chemically bonded to oxygen. This bonding is typically found in functional groups such as carbonyl (C=O) or ether/alcohol (CO) groups. This observation points to the likelihood of partial oxidation or functionalization occurring on the surface. The minor peak observed at 289.66 eV, which accounts for 5.12 % of the total signal, is indicative of O-C=O bonds associated with carboxylate or ester functionalities. This suggests the presence of a small fraction of highly oxidized carbon species, presumably situated on the surface of the material [39].

The O1s XPS spectrum of ECQNF delineates three separate peaks, indicating the presence of various oxygen chemical states on the surface of the material. The prominent peak observed at 533.57 eV, accounting for 74.89 % of the total intensity, is associated with CO bonds found in functional groups such as hydroxyl or ether. This suggests that these functional groups represent the principal oxygen species in the material, as illustrated in Fig. 4. The secondary peak identified at 532.76 eV (46.91 %) correlates with carbonyl oxygen (C=O), which is prevalent in functional groups such as ketones, aldehydes, or esters. This indicates a notable concentration of oxygen species featuring double bonds. Conversely, the least pronounced peak at 531.2 eV (5.2 %) is linked to O-C=O bonds, commonly associated with carboxylate groups or perhaps inorganic oxides. This observation implies a limited occurrence of highly oxidized oxygen species within the sample. The attendance of these peaks proposes that the surface of the material is significantly modified with oxygen-containing functional groups. This modification enhances its chemical versatility and reactivity, making it suitable for a range of applications [36].

The N1s XPS spectrum of ECQNF displays two notable peaks, which delineate the various nitrogen chemical states found at the material's surface. The primary peak, located at 399.47 eV and accounting for 66.81 % of the aggregate nitrogen. The data implies the presence of nitrogen-doped aromatic or graphitic configurations within the material, which facilitate increased electron donation and improved conductivity. Additionally, the secondary peak observed at 403.75 eV, representing 33.19 % of the total, is associated with oxidized nitrogen species [34]. The observed peak signifies the functionalization or interaction of nitrogen with oxygen, likely resulting from surface oxidation mechanisms. Collectively, these peaks suggest the presence of both graphitic nitrogen and oxidized nitrogen species, which play an important role in defining the chemical and electronic characteristics of the material (Fig. 4) [12].

The pHpzc parameter plays a critical role in the adsorption procedure due to its influence on the electrostatic interactions that occur among the adsorbent and the adsorbate, as well as its effect on the pH of the solution. This parameter is defined as the pH level at which the net charge on the surface of the adsorbent is neutral, as illustrated in Fig. 5(a). The pHpzc for ECQNF was determined to be 5.39. This value indicates the pH level at which the net surface charge of the material becomes neutral, influencing its behaviors in various chemical environments [40]. When the pH level falls below the point of zero charge (pHpzc), the adsorbent's surface exhibits a positive charge, thereby favoring the adsorption of anionic species. Conversely, when the pH rises above the pHpzc, the surface becomes negatively charged, resulting in preferential adsorption of cationic species [41].

In the process of adsorption, pH emerges as a significant variable due to its influence on the configuration of the dye and the surface charge characteristics of the adsorbent materials. The effectiveness of the removal process for RB dye was examined across a pH from 2 to 12, as illustrated in Fig. 5(b). At an acidic pH, the presence of amines and hydroxyl groups on the surface of ECQNF can lead to protonation, which subsequently imparts a positive charge to the surface of the nanocomposite. When the pH is below the isoelectric point of 5.39, the adsorption surface exhibits a positive charge. Conversely, at pH levels exceeding 5.39, the surface undergoes a transition to a negative charge, which becomes advantageous for the adsorption of cationic dyes such as RB dye. This pH-dependent charge behavior is critical for understanding the adsorption mechanism and optimizing conditions for dye removal. Under alkaline pH conditions, it can be anticipated that the surface of the adsorbent acquires a negative charge as a result of the deprotonation process affecting its functional groups. Consequently, the adsorption of cationic species in this environment is notably enhanced, largely attributed to the electrostatic interactions that arise among the molecules of RB dye and the negatively charged adsorbent surface. The data exemplified in Fig. 5(b) state that the efficiency of ECQNF in removing RB demonstrated a marked improvement as the pH level of the solution rose from 2 to 8. However, it is significant to note that beyond this pH threshold, the removal efficiency began to decline as pH continued to increase [42]. The removal efficiency reached its peak at 96.6 % at a pH level of 8. It can be concluded that physical processes, particularly electrostatic interactions, primarily govern the adsorption mechanism throughout this process.

Determining the right dosage of the adsorbent is crucial to improving the adsorption process's efficiency. The adsorption capability, expressed in (mg.g−1) for the ECQNF, was assessed in relation to the percentage of RB dye removal (R, %) across a range of dosages from 0.02 to 0.50 g per 25 mL, as demonstrated in Fig. 5(c). Notably, the removal percentage exhibited an increase from 64 to 97.6 % with the increment of the adsorbent dosage [43,44]. Conversely, the adsorption capacity showed a decline from 400 to 24.4 mg.g−1 as the dosage increased. The optimal dosage can be identified at the intersection of the two curves, which occurs at 0.13 g. At this dosage, the adsorption capacity is recorded at 178.99 mg.g−1, alongside a clearance rate of 78.11 % [43,44]. As a result, Fig. 5(c) indicates that this particular value has been chosen for application in subsequent isotherm and kinetics evaluations.

A further aspect to examine when enhancing an adsorption procedure is the concentration of the adsorbate. Research indicates that augmenting the concentration of pollutants influences the adsorption process to a defined degree. In this study, experiments were carried out utilizing RB concentrations between 25 and 610 mg/L, ensuring that all other parameters remained unchanged. The resulting data on removal efficacy and adsorption capability are presented in Fig. 5(d) [45,46]. It is logical to conclude that as the concentration of pollutants rises, the clearance rate will diminish while the adsorption capacity will correspondingly increase. This relationship is further accompanied by a reduction in the removal rate, which occurs due to the saturation of available sites.

Two crucial components of the adsorption mechanism are the identification of the equilibrium point and the length of contact. The goal of the current investigation was to examine how different contact times affect the RB dye ability to adsorb ECQNF particles. This was accomplished by examining multiple contact durations and assessing their respective impacts [47,48]. The influence of interaction duration was examined in experiments characterized by specific parameters: a volume of 25 mL, a pH level of 8, an initial adsorbate concentration set at 500 mg/L, and a sorbent dosage of 0.02 g. post-equilibrium, extending the reaction time does not enhance the adsorption capability, as the available sites on the adsorbent become saturated. Consequently, determining the equilibrium time is crucial for identifying the optimal contact duration for conducting the experiments. Analysis reveals that after approximately one hour, the adsorption efficiency attains a consistent maximum value as the duration of contact extends, as illustrated in Fig. 5(e).

The investigation focused on the influence of temperature variations ranging from 20 to 45 °C. Fig. 5(f) illustrates that the removal efficiency of RB dye increases in correlation with rising temperatures. Specifically, an enhancement in removal efficiency is observed beyond the 20 °C threshold. This phenomenon can be attributed to the dynamics of the adsorption procedure, wherein paraquat molecules transition from the solid phase into the liquid phase. This movement is likely driven by the instability of the hydrogen-bond interactions among the adsorbate and adsorbent, leading to improved adsorption characteristics at elevated temperatures [49].

Adsorption isotherm models, including Langmuir [50], Freundlich [51], Temkin [40], Dubinin–Radushkevich (D-R) [52], and Jossens, are essential tools for understanding and illustrating the adsorption of RB dye onto ECQNF. These models facilitate a complete understanding of the adsorption behavior, underlying mechanisms, and the capacity of the adsorbent. The Langmuir model posits the presence of uniform adsorption sites, emphasizing the concept of monolayer adsorption, and presents critical parameters such as the maximum adsorption capacity (qmax) and the energy of adsorption. Conversely, the Freundlich model acknowledges the heterogeneity of the surface as well as multilayer adsorption, thereby characterizing the intensity of adsorption and the diversity of active sites [53]. The Temkin model systematically examines the thermodynamic characteristics of adsorption processes, specifically focusing on how the adsorption heat fluctuates with changes in coverage. This approach is particularly relevant in scenarios that involve chemisorption. In contrast, the Dubinin-Radushkevich (D-R) model provides a clear distinction among physical and chemical adsorption mechanisms, analyzing the energy distribution among adsorption sites and the degree of heterogeneity, which is especially pertinent for adsorbents with microporous structures. The Jossens model effectively represents intricate adsorption processes that involve interactions between multiple sites and adsorbates, frequently yielding superior data fits for systems where simpler models are insufficient. Utilizing these models allows for a comprehensive characterization of the adsorption of RB dye onto ECQNF, facilitating the optimization of adsorbent design, assessment of adsorption efficiency, and an enhanced understanding of the fundamental mechanisms, all of which are essential for practical applications in wastewater treatment.

Employing adsorption isotherm models, especially the Langmuir isotherm, serves as a valuable approach for depicting the adsorption process of RB dye on ECQNF. These models facilitate a comprehensive analysis of the underlying adsorption mechanism, the belongings of the surface, and the effectiveness of the adsorbent, as detailed in Table S4. The Langmuir model posits that adsorption happens in a monolayer fashion on a uniform surface characterized by a limited number of active sites. This model facilitates the accurate assessment of critical parameters, including the maximum adsorption capability (qe) and the Langmuir constant (KL), which signifies the strength of the adsorption affinity [50]. The reported adsorption capacity of RB onto ECQNF, quantified at qe = 522.2 mg/g, reflects the material's remarkable capability to capture substantial amounts of dye, thereby underscoring its potential effectiveness for dye elimination in wastewater treatment processes. Additionally, the Langmuir constant (KL = 0.019 L/mg) suggests a moderate affinity, which implies a balance between the robustness of the adsorption interaction and the likelihood of desorption, as illustrated in Fig. 6(a). The Langmuir model serves a dual purpose: it confirms the considerable adsorption capacity of ECQNF and aids in the fine-tuning of both adsorbent configuration and operational parameters [54].

The implementation of adsorption isotherm models, with a focus on the Freundlich isotherm, presents notable benefits for characterizing the adsorption of RB dye onto ECQNF. This model is mainly suited for depicting adsorption processes occurring on heterogeneous surfaces and offers valuable information regarding both the intensity and capacity of adsorption. In contrast to the Langmuir model, which presupposes homogenous adsorption sites, the Freundlich model incorporates the concept of heterogeneous surface energies and the possibility of multilayer adsorption. In the setting of the adsorption of RB dye onto ECQNF, the Freundlich constant n = 2.25 implies that the adsorption procedure is favorable. The presented value indicates that there are considerable interactions taking place between the adsorbent and the dye molecules, which is substantiated by the data in Table S5. The Freundlich constant, KF = 55.27(mg/g)(L/mg)1/n, serves as a metric for the adsorption capacity at unit concentration, thereby highlighting the effectiveness of ECQNF in the removal of RB [51]. The Freundlich model, through the inclusion of specific parameters, elucidates the material's proficiency in managing diverse concentrations of RB dye. This underscores its flexibility and efficiency in practical contexts, especially within wastewater treatment situations characterized by complex and heterogeneous systems [55].

The Dubinin–Radushkevich (D-R) isotherm model presents important benefits in elucidating the adsorption process of RB dye onto ECQNF. Specifically, it effectively distinguishes among physical and chemical adsorption processes. The model emphasizes the energy distribution across heterogeneous surfaces, thereby yielding valuable perspectives on the characteristics and mechanisms of adsorption. The adsorption capacity (QDR = 494.88mg/g) underscores the exceptional capability of ECQNF in eliminating RB dye, particularly effective at low concentration levels, which underlines its operational efficiency. Additionally, the D-R constant (KDR = 1.02 × 10−4 mol2/kJ2) facilitates the assessment of the mean free energy of adsorption (Ea = 32.6kJ/mol). This value suggests that chemisorption predominates as the mechanism of adsorption, as it surpasses the threshold of 8 kJ/mol, which is typically indicative of physical adsorption processes. The comprehension of adsorption energy offers a thermodynamic viewpoint, shedding light on the stability and reliability of the adsorption mechanism. Consequently, the Dubinin-Radushkevich (D-R) model serves as a valuable tool in enhancing the design of adsorbents, thereby maximizing efficiency in applications such as wastewater treatment [52].

The Temkin isotherm model offers distinct benefits in depicting the adsorption dynamics of RB dye on ECQNF, especially in terms of elucidating the thermodynamic parameters and interaction mechanisms involved in the adsorption process. In difference to alternative isotherm models, the Temkin model accounts for the interactions among the adsorbent and the adsorbate. It implies that as surface coverage increases, the heat of adsorption falls linearly; this pattern is explained by the repulsive interactions between the molecules of the adsorbate. The Temkin constant bT = 21.23J/mol represents the heat of adsorption, signifying a moderate level of adsorption energy that facilitates effective adsorption while avoiding substantial energy barriers. Additionally, the equilibrium binding constant KT = 0.422L/mol emphasizes the robustness of the bonds formed between the adsorbate and adsorbent, representing that the adsorption procedure is promising. The Temkin model, through its inclusion of specific parameters, offers significant insights into the energetic components of adsorption as well as the degree of surface heterogeneity. This renders the model an essential instrument for analyzing and enhancing the effectiveness of ECQNF in the removal of RB dye. This is especially pertinent in dynamic systems where the balance between thermodynamic stability and energy efficiency is vital for the effective treatment of wastewater in real-world applications.

The Jossens isotherm model presents several notable benefits in representing the adsorption dynamics of RB dye onto ECQNF. It effectively addresses the intricacies inherent in adsorption mechanisms that encompass both multi-site and multilayer adsorption phenomena. The constant K = 11.07 reflects the equilibrium binding strength, indicating the strong interaction between RB dye and ECQNF. The parameter J = 0.066 offers valuable information regarding the variability of adsorption sites and the range of adsorption energies present on the surface. This model proves to be particularly effective in characterizing systems where more straightforward models, such as Langmuir or Freundlich, fall short in delivering precise representations, primarily due to the complexity introduced by various adsorption mechanisms or non-uniform surface characteristics. The Jossens model effectively incorporates various complexities, leading to a deeper comprehension of the interactions between RB dye molecules and ECQNF. This enhanced understanding is instrumental in refining the performance of adsorbents and tailoring operational settings. Consequently, the model holds significant promise for the development of sophisticated and efficient systems aimed at the removal of dyes in real-world wastewater treatment scenarios.

The study examined the adsorption of RB dye onto ECQNF at various temperatures (25, 35, and 45 °C), utilizing several isotherm models: Langmuir, Freundlich, Dubinin–Radushkevich (D-R), Temkin, and Jossens. The findings, detailed in Table S5 and Fig. S1, revealed that the Langmuir model indicated an increase in maximum adsorption capacity (qm) from 522.2 mg/g at 25 °C to 612.2 mg/g at 45 °C. This increase was accompanied by a rising Langmuir constant (KL) and a decreasing separation factor (RL), suggesting that adsorption became more favorable at higher temperatures. In addition, the Freundlich model showed enhancements in both adsorption capacity (KF) and adsorption intensity (n), reflecting stronger interactions as the temperature increased. The D-R model indicated that the adsorption energy (Ea) rose from 32.6 kJ/mol to 34.8 kJ/mol, indicating a chemisorption mechanism, further supported by an increase in adsorption capacity (QDR). According to the Temkin model, both the binding energy constant (bT) and the equilibrium constant (KT) increased, reinforcing the idea of stronger interactions between adsorbate and adsorbent at elevated temperatures. The Jossens model demonstrated an increase in the adsorption constant (K) and a slight heterogeneity factor (J), suggesting a varied adsorption surface. Fig. S1 illustrated that the Langmuir and Freundlich models were the most appropriate fits, showing characteristics of both monolayer and multilayer adsorption, with a significant increase in adsorption capacity (qe) as temperature rose, indicative of an endothermic process. The consistently higher binding constants and adsorption energies across all models affirm that interactions and diffusion enhanced with increased temperature. The alterations in adsorption curves pointed to quicker kinetics, and the D-R model verified the chemisorption hypothesis, highlighting ECQNF's effectiveness as an adsorbent for RB dye removal. Overall, the results indicate that elevated temperatures enhance adsorption efficiency, making ECQNF particularly effective for RB dye elimination due to its potent adsorption capabilities and significant increases in capacity at higher temperatures.

Adsorption kinetic models, including Pseudo-First-Order [56], Pseudo-Second-Order [57], Intraparticle Diffusion [58], and Elovich [59], provide substantial benefits in elucidating the adsorption of RB dye onto ECQNF by delivering comprehensive insights into the mechanisms of adsorption and the kinetics that govern the rate-controlling processes. The Pseudo-First-Order model posits that the rates of adsorption are directly related to the availability of unoccupied sites for adsorption. This model is particularly applicable to systems where physical interactions prevail, facilitating swift initial adsorption processes. Furthermore, it provides key parameters that assist in forecasting the initial rates of adsorption. The Pseudo-Second-Order model exhibits a higher degree of accuracy in the context of chemisorption processes, wherein the adsorption process is characterized by robust interactions or chemical bonding. This model effectively quantifies both the equilibrium adsorption capability and the rates of adsorption. Its predictive capabilities often correspond closely with the adsorption of RB dye onto ECQNF. The Intraparticle Diffusion model serves to determine if the diffusion of dye molecules into the pores of electrospun carbon quantum nanofibers (ECQNF) represents the rate-limiting factor. This analysis offers valuable understanding of the multi-stage adsorption mechanisms, spanning from surface adsorption to internal diffusion. Such insights are essential for enhancing the design and configuration of adsorbent pore structures [60]. The Elovich model, which is well-suited for heterogeneous surfaces, describes the decline in the adsorption rate over time as surface sites reach saturation. This model provides information on initial adsorption rates and chemisorption dynamics on the complex surface of ECQNF, as shown in Table S4. When used together with other models, these analyses contribute to a thorough understanding of the adsorption procedure, aiding in the optimization of ECQNF for the effective, efficient, and maintainable elimination of RB dye in practical wastewater treatment applications, as illustrated in Fig. 6(b).

The Pseudo-First-Order kinetic model is beneficial for elucidating the adsorption behavior of RB dye onto ECQNF, as it offers significant understanding regarding the dynamics and underlying mechanisms of the adsorption process, especially in scenarios where physical adsorption plays a predominant role. The model operates on the premise that the rate of adsorption is directly related to the quantity of available, unoccupied adsorption sites. This relationship positions the model as particularly effective in characterizing the initial dynamics of the adsorption process. The rate constant K1 = 0.05min−1 × 10−2 for the adsorption of RB dye onto ECQNF indicates a significant rate for the adsorption process, underscoring the swift initial absorption of dye molecules by ECQNF. By applying this model to experimental data, investigators can quantify the rate at which equilibrium is achieved and evaluate the effectiveness of ECQNF in sequestering RB dye from water-based solutions. The straightforward nature of the Pseudo-First-Order model renders it an effective instrument for evaluating systems primarily governed by physisorption [56]. The Pseudo-Second-Order kinetic model provides significant benefits for elucidating the adsorption behavior of RB dye on ECQNF. This model is particularly effective in situations where the adsorption process is primarily characterized by chemisorption, which comprises interactions driven by valence forces or the sharing of electrons among the adsorbent and the adsorbate [57]. This model postulates that the rate of adsorption is directly proportional to the square of the obtainable adsorption sites. This assumption indicates a greater applicability to systems in which robust chemical bonding or strong interactions are influential, as outlined in Table S6. The adsorption of RB dye onto ECQNF is characterized by a rate constant (K2) of 9.799 × 10−5 g.mg−1 min−1 and an equilibrium adsorption capability (qe) of 523.6 mg/g, both of which underscore the remarkable efficiency and substantial adsorption capability of ECQNF. The elevated qe value illustrates the superior affinity of ECQNF for RB, while the K2 value sheds light on the kinetics and velocity of the adsorption device. The Pseudo-Second-Order model's precision in predicting equilibrium behavior and its relevance to chemisorption make it a reliable tool for optimizing adsorbent performance, ensuring effective RB dye removal, and tailoring adsorption systems for practical wastewater treatment applications [61].

The Intraparticle Diffusion model serves as a appreciated tool for elucidating the adsorption mechanism of RB dye onto ECQNF. It offers a comprehensive understanding of the adsorption process, which occurs in multiple stages, and effectively highlights the steps that dictate the rate of the overall adsorption phenomenon. In the case of RB dye adsorption onto ECQNF, the intraparticle diffusion rate constant (Ki = 60.012mg.g−1 min1/2) signifies the rate at which diffusion occurs in the adsorbent's pores, thereby indicating that the intraparticle diffusion process is relatively swift. The constant X = 18.6 mg/g, which signifies the intercept, offers valuable information regarding the role of boundary layer effects and the adsorption occurring on the external surface prior to the prevalence of diffusion processes [62]. This two-tiered analysis facilitates the distinction between surface adsorption and pore diffusion as the primary mechanisms of control. The model's capability to discern the specific phases of adsorption contributes to a deeper comprehension of ECQNF's efficacy. This understanding aids in the refinement of its pore architecture, surface characteristics, and operational conditions to enhance the removal of RB dye. Consequently, the Intraparticle Diffusion model emerges as a pivotal instrument for progressing adsorption methodologies within wastewater treatment contexts [58]. The Elovich kinetic model proves to be beneficial in depicting the adsorption procedure of RB dye onto ECQNF. This model is particularly effective for representing systems characterized by a predominance of chemisorption on heterogeneous surfaces, as well as scenarios where the rate of adsorption diminishes over time, primarily due to the saturation of available sites. The parameter β = 115.18g/mg serves as the desorption constant, which quantifies the energy necessary for adsorbate molecules to surmount barriers and disengage from the surface, thereby indicating a stable adsorption mechanism. Concurrently, the parameter α = 0.0077mg.g−1 min−1 denotes the initial adsorption rate, illustrating the swift assimilation of RB dye molecules by ECQNF at the outset of the adsorption process. The Elovich model offers significant analytical perspectives on the kinetics of adsorption occurring on heterogeneous surfaces characterized by varying energy sites, with a particular emphasis on the chemisorption process. Its capacity to delineate time-dependent adsorption rates facilitates an in-depth comprehension of the interaction dynamics between RB dye and ECQNF. Through the alignment of experimental data with this model, researchers are able to enhance the efficacy of ECQNF for real-world uses, thereby facilitating effective and prolonged elimination of dyes in wastewater treatment contexts [59]. The initial phase of the process involves mass transfer: i) During this phase, RB dye molecules migrate from the majority solution by traversing through the film-like margin layer; ii) Subsequently, in the second phase, pore diffusion characterized by Fickian diffusion occurs alongside surface diffusion; iii) Ultimately, the final stage involves the adsorption of paraquat on available active sites [63]. During the rapid adsorption phase, a series of physical and chemical interactions contribute to the adsorption of paraquat. These interactions include the formation of hydrogen bonds, π–π interactions, potential stacking, dipole-dipole interactions, and the possibility of n–π interactions [46,64]. Considering the available RB dye cations, it is expected that dipole-dipole and n-π interactions will occur. However, the effectiveness of this mechanism is also contingent upon the type of adsorbent, as illustrated in Fig. 6(c, d).

The kinetics of RB dye adsorption onto ECQNF were examined at varying temperatures (25, 35, and 45 °C) using four different kinetic models: Pseudo-First-Order, Pseudo-Second-Order, Intraparticle Diffusion, and Elovich. The detailed findings can be referenced in Table S6 and Fig. S2. The Pseudo-First-Order model, which includes the rate constant K1, displayed a declining trend with temperature, moving from 0.05 at 25 °C to 0.028 at 45 °C. This indicates that physical adsorption is not the primary mechanism at play. Conversely, the Pseudo-Second-Order model, which is predicated on chemisorption, showed a more accurate representation of the data, as indicated by the increasing equilibrium adsorption capacity (qe) that rose from 523.6 mg/g at 25 °C to 610.6 mg/g at 45 °C, suggesting that higher temperatures enhance adsorption efficiency. The rate constant K2 also decreased with rising temperatures, further implying that the adsorption process is governed by chemical interactions rather than rapid diffusion. The Intraparticle Diffusion model, which analyzes the transport of RB dye molecules into the pores of ECQNF, indicated increasing diffusion rate constants (Ki) from 60.012 mg/g·min1/2 at 25 °C to 63.6 mg/g·min1/2 at 45 °C, thereby suggesting improved diffusion within pores at elevated temperatures. Additionally, a significant increase in boundary layer thickness was observed, reinforcing the notion that both surface adsorption and film diffusion play roles in this overall process. The Elovich model, which is utilized to describe chemisorption on non-uniform surfaces, reported a decrease in the adsorption rate (α) from 0.0077 mg/g·min at 25 °C to 0.005 mg/g·min at 45 °C, coupled with an increase in the desorption constant (β), indicating stronger interactions between the adsorbate and adsorbent at higher temperatures. According to Fig. S2, the Pseudo-Second-Order model closely aligns with the experimental results, affirming that chemisorption is the primary process involved. Moreover, the Intraparticle Diffusion model suggests a synergistic action of surface adsorption and pore diffusion. The observation that adsorption capacity increases with temperature confirms the endothermic nature of the adsorption process, positioning ECQNF as an effective adsorbent for the removal of RB dye.

The investigation into the adsorptive removal efficacy of ECQNF for RB dye was conducted across a temperature range of 293 to 318 K. The results presented in Table S7 indicate that there is an enhancement in adsorption capacities with rising temperatures. This phenomenon can be attributed to the intensified interactions between RB dye molecules and the ECQNF as temperature increases, providing insight into the thermal dynamics governing the adsorption process [18]. To gain a deeper insight into the adsorption process, thermodynamic parameters were computed utilizing Eq. S9. In this context, T(K) represents the absolute temperature, and R (J/mol K) denotes the universal gas constant. The values for the enthalpy change (ΔHo) and the entropy change (ΔS0) were inferred through the graphical representation of ln Kc against 1/T, as illustrated in Fig. 7(a) [65]. The determined enthalpy change of 117.2 kJ/mol, classified as a high value, reflects a significant interaction among RB dye molecules and ECQNF. This finding implies that the adsorption process is endothermic, thereby supporting the spontaneity of the adsorption as indicated in Eq. S10. The value of ΔSo (399.07 J/mol.K) indicates an increase in the system's entropy following the adsorption procedure. This enhancement in randomness aligns with conditions that favor spontaneous adsorption, as illustrated in Fig. 7 (b, c). The calculated negative standard free energy (ΔGo) values derived from Eq. S9 provide additional evidence supporting the notion that RB dye molecules were adsorbed onto ECQNF spontaneously within the investigated temperature range. Furthermore, the adsorption energy, determined using the Arrhenius equation, was found to be 35.7 kJ/mol, representing that the procedure of adsorption can be classified as chemisorption, as outlined in Eq. S11 [66].

Analyzing the adsorption mechanism of RB dye on the ECQNF adsorbent is crucial for optimizing the adsorption process and enhancing the efficiency of the nanofibers in removing RB dye. The adsorption of RB dye onto nanofibrous materials is influenced by various factors, including the solution conditions (such as pH and temperature), the characteristics of the nanofibers (including porosity, functional groups, surface area, and morphology), and the properties of the dye itself (such as its cationic or anionic form and molecular size) [67]. The process of removing dye from water-based solutions through ECQNF involves several key interactions, including hydrogen bonding, π–π stacking, van der Waals forces, electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic interactions, and pore filling. The effectiveness of each communication is contingent upon the specific functional groups present on the nanofibers' surfaces, their morphological characteristics, and the properties of any additives incorporated into the nanofibers. The functional groups serve as active binding sites for the adsorption of RB dye. In this context, the electrostatic attractions between the positively charged molecule of RB dye and the negatively charged elements of ECQNF play a significant role. Additionally, the π–π stacking interactions occurring between the CC bonds of RB and the π-electrons within the aromatic structures further facilitate the adsorption mechanism. The interaction between hydrogen bonds formed by -OH and/or -COOH functional groups on the surfaces of ECQNF and the nitrogen atoms in RB dye, alongside the contributions from Yoshida hydrogen bonding. This indicates that the incorporation of ECQNF leads to an increased concentration of oxygen-rich functional groups, which in turn promotes electrostatic interactions and π–π electron coupling [43,68].

The process of RB dye adsorption onto ECQNF can be analyzed through three central components. Initially, the adsorption dynamics of RB dye appear to be strongly affected by the electrostatic interactions involving the positively charged RB molecule, characterized by its amine group structure (–N+). The surface of the membrane exhibits a capacity to acquire negative charges, attributed to the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups (-COO−). Additionally, the inherent high surface energy combined with the extensive specific surface area of the nanofiber membranes suggests that physical adsorption could be a crucial mechanism influencing dye uptake. Furthermore, a conjugation interaction may occur between the vinyl (or carbonyl) functional groups on the membrane surface and the delocalized π bonds in the dye molecules. Fig.S3 displays the FT-IR spectra of the ECQNF and the RB@ECQNF, revealing significant changes in the vibrational characteristics of functional groups post-RB adsorption. These alterations indicate a successful interaction between the substances. Notably, the hydroxyl (-OH) stretching band, which falls within the 3200–3500 cm−1 range, exhibits a decrease in intensity. This reduction suggests the formation of hydrogen bonds between RB and the ECQNF. Additionally, peaks associated with CH stretching, located between 2800 and 3000 cm−1, show slight shifts and variations in intensity, which can be attributed to the interactions occurring with the aromatic groups of RB [69]. Moreover, the carbonyl (C=O) stretching absorption, situated approximately in the range of 1700–1750 cm−1, exhibits both a shift and a reduction in intensity, highlighting its significance in the adsorption of RB dye. Similarly, the CC aromatic stretching vibrations, observed within the range of 1500–1600 cm−1, show marked alterations, indicative of π-π interactions with RB dye. The observed changes in intensity within the spectral bands corresponding to CN stretching and NH deformation (1200–1400 cm−1) as well as C-O-C ether groups (1000–1250 cm−1) suggest the occurrence of chemical interactions. Furthermore, the fingerprint region (600–900 cm−1) indicates additional structural alterations linked to the binding of RB dye. Together, these spectral changes corroborate that the adsorption of RB dye is facilitated by mechanisms such as hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and π-π stacking with the functional groups present in the ECQNF. Additionally, the interaction of RB dye with ECQNF leads to a significant reduction in porosity. In particular, the measurements for surface area and pore volume reveal that a portion of RB dye is absorbed through the porosity of the membrane.

The impact of ionic strength was examined to further elucidate the electrostatic interactions occurring between RB dye molecules and ECQNF. It is important to note that the presence of ionic strength, which results from the addition of different salts to the dye solution, can substantially alter these electrostatic interactions due to the screening effect. From a theoretical perspective, the presence of attractive electrostatic interactions suggests that the introduction of salt into the dye solution could potentially diminish the adsorption capacity. This research explores the influence of varying concentrations of NaCl on the adsorption efficiency of ECQNF by systematically adding different quantities of NaCl to the RB dye solution. Fig. 8 indicates that the efficacy of ECQNF in removing RB dye diminished upon the introduction of NaCl, with this decrease becoming more pronounced as the NaCl concentration increased. This observation implies that the presence of salt may induce an electrostatic screening effect, leading to the desorption of RB dye molecules from the surface of the adsorbent back into the dye solution. Consequently, it can be inferred that electrostatic interactions are the primary mechanism governing the adsorption of RB dye onto ECQNF [70].

The reusability of an adsorbent significantly contributes to its overall value and cost-effectiveness in adsorption applications. In this context, the adsorbent's capability for regeneration, along with its efficiency in removing contaminants, is of paramount importance. To evaluate the reusability of ECQNF, a series of eight adsorption and desorption cycles were conducted. This process involved the elution of ECQNF using an ethanol aimed at effectively removing all RB dye molecules that had adhered to its surface [71]. Fig. 9(a) illustrates that the ECQNF demonstrated a consistent ability to eliminate the RB dye, exhibiting only a minor reduction in efficacy from 98.4 to 84.2 % after undergoing eight successive adsorption-desorption cycles. This observation indicates the presence of reversible and stable adsorption sites within the material. Furthermore, an XRD analysis was conducted on the ECQNF material after three testing cycles, revealing that its structural integrity remained intact, as depicted in Fig. 9(b).

The maximum sorption capacities of the ECQNF sorbent are evaluated against various values obtained from the literature, as presented in Table S8, which also includes the corresponding optimal operating parameters specified. Though the variations in experimental conditions complicate direct contrasts of sorption efficacy, this criterion remains suitable for a comprehensive assessment of the potential of these materials. The ECQNF sorbent exhibits significant advantages due to its rapid kinetics. Its pronounced capacity to interact with RB dye indicates its potential application in wastewater treatment, mainly for the effective removal of this substance from aquatic systems [72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modelling experimental designs and analyzing response surfaces

ce:section.ce:para:
The ANOVA examination was employed to statistically corroborate the findings obtained from the investigates conducted within the framework of the BBD methodology, as illustrated in Table 1 [73,74]. Table 2 presents the findings from the ANOVA examination. The model's F-values are recorded at 59.14, while the p-values concerning the adsorption of RB dye are consistently lower than 0.0001. These outcomes suggest that the projected quadratic model is statistically significant and effectively clarifies the results obtained, as detailed in Table 3 [31,39]. Furthermore, the R2 values demonstrate a robust relationship of 0.9846 for RB dye when comparing the observed and predicted values, which is near the ideal value of 1. In the process of eliminating RB dye, model terms yielding p-values below 0.05 are considered statistically significant. Any variables in the second-order polynomial model with p-values higher than 0.05 were removed in order to improve the BBD model's fit. The equation representing the primary term associated with RB dye is presented in Eq. 5, alongside its coded counterpart in Eq. 6.

The equation is applicable for forecasting results based on different levels of each variable, utilizing coding systems to denote the variables [32]. In analyses, high values of variables are typically represented by +1, while low values are indicated by −1. By assessing the coefficients associated with each variable, this encoded equation serves as a tool for evaluating the interrelationships and effects among the variables in question [30].
