The potential exists to predict the results for individual values of every variable through the application of the real variable calculation [75,76]. Considering that the constants have been modified to reflect the units associated with each variable and that the intercept is not positioned centrally within the design interplanetary, it is inappropriate to utilize this equation for determining the relative meaning of the variables [38].

The visual correlation between anticipated and observed values is presented in Fig. 10 . The ECQNF was applied to eliminate the effects of RB dye. In addition, several analytical techniques were utilized, such as assessing residuals in relation to the run, evaluating very well-studied residuals, constructing a BOX-COX plot, and performing a perturbation plot analysis. The statistical dependability of the model is demonstrated through the noteworthy alignment observed between the predicted and actual values, as shown in Figs. 10(a, b). Furthermore, Figs. 10(c, d) illustrate the correlation among the residuals and the dynamics of the RB dye adsorption procedure [23]. The extensive dispersion of data points around the zero mark, along with their significant clustering in that region, indicates the model's dependability and efficacy. Through the comparison of predicted response values with the observed actual values, it becomes possible to identify any latent or unforeseen values or designs that the model might not have captured. The Box-Cox curves illustrated in Fig. 10(e) serve as an analytical instrument for determining the most suitable power transformation purpose for the response variable. The minimum point on the Box-Cox graph signifies the optimal Lambda value, correlating with the minimal residual sum of squares in the revised model, Lambda. The ideal value of Lambda, defined as the minimal residual sum of squares within the modified model, is located at the minimum point on the Box-Cox graph [24,33]. The graphs illustrating the model's appropriateness indicate its strong performance. One variable was altered while keeping all additional factors constant to evaluate the yield response. The correlation between pH levels and the duration of the adsorption procedure highlighted the rapid effect of these factors on adsorption capability [77,78]. The factors of time, pH, and dosage all exhibited a positive influence on the adsorption capacity throughout the adsorption process, as demonstrated in Fig. 10(f). Analysis revealed that the ideal situations for adsorption were originate at a pH of 8, with a dosage of 0.2 g considered optimal, and a contact duration of 100 min being most effective [79].

3D surface response plots were used to explore the important interactions between the factors under investigation to obtain a thorough grasp of their impact on the removal of RB dye. Fig. 11(a) validates the effects of variations in pH and ECQNF dosage on the adsorption capability of RB dye, with each alteration being observed over a duration of 100 min. The adsorption capacity for RB dye exhibited a notable increase when the pH was elevated from 2 to 5, as illustrated in Fig. 11(b). Furthermore, this capacity continued to enhance at a pH of 8, with the optimal conditions identified as a pH of 8 combined with an adsorbent dose of 0.2 g for a solvent volume of 25 mL. The pH of 8 emerged as the most actual parameter, attributed to the positive charge of RB dye alongside the point of zero charge (pHpzc) of ECQNF, which is 5.39. It was observed that conducting the adsorption process at pH levels surpassing 5.39 proved to be beneficial for the overall adsorption efficacy. The effects of manipulating the dosage and exposure time of ECQNF on the adsorption capacity of RB dye are presented in Fig. 11(b). The findings further indicate that the adsorption capacity increased with prolonged contact duration, reaching its maximum at a dosage of 0.2 g of adsorbent after 100 min of contact time. Fig. 11(c) details the influence of different durations and pH levels of ECQNF on its capacity to absorb RB dye. Notably, as the pH values increased, especially in the alkaline range, the absorption capacity demonstrated an initial rise from 0.02 to 0.5 g, followed by a subsequent decrease. Furthermore, the absorption efficacy was positively correlated with the length of contact time, indicating an increase as the duration extended.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The validation of models and the desirability approach
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12(a) illustrates the optimal predictable values for the investigational parameters required to achieve the highest adsorption capability. Meanwhile, Fig. 12(b) presents the outcomes of the desirability purpose analysis. The experimental response was evaluated by varying each factor within defined boundaries, including initial concentration (C0) between 25 and 600 mg.L−1, temperature settings ranging from 20 to 45 °C, and contact duration spanning from 5 to 100 min. The findings of the experiment corresponded significantly with the anticipated outcomes [24]. This indicates that the model was both suitable and adequate for the task at hand. As illustrated in Fig. 12(c), two compliance experiments were then executed employing the optimized input parameters [25,26]. This experiment demonstrated the precision and efficacy of the BBD in conjunction with the desired function for establishing the optimal adsorption parameters that yield maximum output. Consequently, further investigations focusing on isotherms, kinetics, and thermodynamics were conducted under these optimal conditions [37,38].

The electrospinning technique has been effectively employed to generate electrospun carbon quantum dots nanofibers (ECQNF). These nanofibers, integrated with CQDs and formulated from chitosan and polyvinyl alcohol, serve as a novel and well-organized adsorbent material. The distinctiveness of this study lies in the development of a multifunctional adsorbent tailored for the efficient elimination of cationic dyes, specifically RB dye. The evaluation of membrane performance involved conducting multiple batch adsorption experiments utilizing RB dye as a representative pollutant. These tests specifically analyzed various factors, including pH levels, contact duration, initial pollutant concentration, and the amount of adsorbent used. The findings designated that the adsorption of RB dye is influenced by the concentration of the dye, with the membrane exhibiting a maximum adsorption capability of 522 mg/g, as determined by the Langmuir model. The uptake of RB dye demonstrated a clear dependence on pH, exhibiting an increase as pH levels rose, peaking at pH = 8. This trend can be primarily qualified to the formation of electrostatic interactions, although it is important to acknowledge that other mechanisms may also be involved. Furthermore, the adsorption of RB dye demonstrated endothermic characteristics, indicating that the adsorption capacity enhanced with rising temperatures. The kinetic data aligned closely with the pseudo-second-order model, which showed a strong correlation. Additionally, the computed adsorption capability was in close agreement with the investigational values. Additionally, the introduction of salt ions had a significant impact on the adsorption capacity, resulting in a measurable decrease. Moreover, the ECQNF exhibited stable performance and maintained its chemical composition, which was corroborated by consistent results from XRD analyses conducted before and after six cycles of reuse. To enhance the efficiency of adsorption, a Box Behnken design (BBD) was employed for optimization purposes. This research is innovative in that it explores the creation of a novel composite material integrating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with chitosan, alongside polyvinyl alcohol electrospun membranes, which serve as effective adsorbents for cationic dyes.
